summary: Gruber argues that Microsoft's unprecedented year-over-year revenue decline, particularly the 29% drop in Windows division revenue, signals the beginning of a long structural decline rather than a temporary economic blip. He points to NPD data showing Apple capturing 91% of the $1,000+ retail computer market as evidence that Microsoft has lost the most lucrative market segment. The core of his argument is that Microsoft has lost computer enthusiasts — the leading indicators who influence broader consumer behavior — drawing a parallel to how GM lost car enthusiasts before losing mainstream buyers. He criticizes Microsoft's leadership for responding to Apple's gains with cheap-PC marketing rather than building something genuinely competitive, and notes that Windows 7's ambition extends only to being better than Vista, not better than Mac OS X. Gruber concludes this isn't an aberration but the first fiscal evidence of a decline that began years earlier.
key_points: Apple's share of the $1,000+ retail computer market grew from 66% to 91% in eighteen months, while Microsoft's Windows division revenue declined 29% year-over-year
Microsoft has lost computer enthusiasts — the leading indicator demographic whose preferences eventually shape mainstream buying — paralleling GM's decline in the auto industry
Windows 7's goal was merely to be better than Vista, not to compete with Mac OS X, revealing Microsoft has conceded the enthusiast market
Microsoft's leadership treats Apple's gains as a joke, responding with price-focused marketing while ignoring that their platform is perceived as second-rate
Microsoft is becoming the Wal-Mart of computing — winning on volume and price — but that's not a business model compatible with a software company's margins
money_quotes: People who love computers overwhelmingly prefer to use a Mac today. Microsoft's core problem is that they have lost the hearts of computer enthusiasts.
Microsoft is looking ever more so like the digital equivalent of General Motors. Car enthusiasts lost interest in GM's cars long before regular people did; the same is happening with Windows.
Today, though, Microsoft is increasingly left only with customers whose priority is price.

If Microsoft were a healthy, functional, competitive company willing and able to honestly assess its own shortcomings — like the Microsoft of the '90s that conquered the entire industry — their goal would have been to make something not just better than Vista, but better than anything else on the market, including Mac OS X.
No one with a pair of eyes thinks such machines are of comparable quality to Apple MacBooks. Even without turning the machines on, anyone can see the difference in design and build quality.
It seems clear that Microsoft's stance on the Mac's sales growth is that there's nothing wrong with Windows or right with the Mac, but rather that there's something wrong with Mac users.

They still dominate in terms of unit-sale market share, yes, but not because people don't recognize Windows as second-rate, but because they don't care, in the same way millions of people buy metric tons of second-rate products from Wal-Mart every hour of every day.

I'm not arguing that Microsoft will collapse. They're too big, too established for that to happen. I simply think that their results this quarter were not an aberration, but rather the first fiscal evidence of a long, slow decline that began several years ago.
key_insight: Microsoft's real crisis isn't a bad quarter but a structural loss of the enthusiast class whose preferences are leading indicators for the broader market, leaving Microsoft competing only on price in a business that depends on margins.
